slides.title: The Master Control Panel 🎛️
slides.content:
Every professional TypeScript project has a `tsconfig.json` file at its root.

This file is the absolute control center. It tells the TypeScript compiler exactly how strict to be, what features to support, and where to output the final JavaScript files.

slides.title: A Minimal tsconfig
slides.content: You can auto-generate a massive default file by running `tsc --init`, but here are the most critical settings you will actually care about:

quizData.question: What does the `"outDir": "./dist"` compiler option do?

options.text: It deletes the dist folder entirely.
It tells the compiler to take all the newly compiled `.js` files and place them exclusively into the `dist` directory.
It downloads external JavaScript libraries into the dist folder.

quizData.explanation: `outDir` (Output Directory) cleanly separates your pristine TypeScript source code from the compiled output JavaScript files.

slides.title: Strict Mode: The Enforcer 👮
slides.content:
The absolute most important setting is `"strict": true`.
This one boolean natively turns on about 8 different strict-checking rules concurrently, including `noImplicitAny` and `strictNullChecks`.

quizData.question: If you set `"strict": false`, TypeScript completely ignores all explicit type annotations and runs exactly like Python.
quizData.explanation: TypeScript still checks types, but it becomes dangerously lenient. For instance, it will happily allow you to use `null` anywhere or forget to type your parameters (defaulting them silently to `any`).

slides.title: Targeting the Web 🕸️
slides.content:
The `"target"` option is magical.

You can explicitly write hyper-modern TypeScript using the absolute newest features available today. But if you set `"target": "ES5"`, the compiler will aggressively rewrite your code backwards into old, ugly, highly compatible JavaScript that runs perfectly on browsers from 10 years ago!

slides.title: Quest Complete! 🛡️
slides.content: You now understand the engine that elegantly powers the compiler. Remember, always keep `"strict": true` gracefully enabled for the best, most flawlessly secure developer experience!
